patient_utterances: Hi.

[Clinician asked: "Hi there. It's Doctor Smith from Babylon. Hi Doctor Smith. Hi, can you confirm your name, date of birth, and your home address please?"] Hi Doctor Smith. Yes. My name is Mary Jo. Date of birth is January first, nineteen eighty.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] And my address is uh, sixty Sloane Avenue, London.
[Clinician asked: "OK, I, are you in a private place where you can have a consultation today?"] Absolutely.

[Clinician asked: "What can I do for you?"] So, a couple of things. I, I, think I may have, overdone my workouts, and now my knees are hurting, and they're clicking a lot, and it's starting to worry me. Um, I can't run very far, um, any more without just feeling, pain in my knees. And then, sometimes I also get this weird tingling in my toes. Um. So yeah, that's, that's it. I'm just concerned and I figured you know I'd call and, see if you can, help me figure out what's going on.
[Clinician asked: "OK, that's fine. So when do you think it started?"] So probably, it's been about two weeks.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Um, and, any joint problems before that?"] You know not, not really. I mean I play a lot of sports. Um, and I had a , like I play some ice hockey, and I had this injury a few months ago. You know I just like, fell on the ice and, and, you know had a, huge bruise but, I think that's about it.

[Clinician asked: "OK, So, um, at the moment, just tell me about the exercise that you're doing, normally."] So I'm training, well, I'm currently training for a marathon, um, that's gonna happen in, June. And so, I'm trying to run like. I mean right now, I'm actually not even doing such long runs, maybe like seven or eight miles. And so, I started about uh, maybe a month ago.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Uh, I've done like, you know, four or five miles, and I've just done like a couple of seven, mile runs. And then uh, uh, and then you know I go to the gym like few times a week and, kinda do my, usual routine.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Um, but, yeah, I think the only thing that's really different has, have been, the runs, the longer runs.
[Clinician asked: "And, have you had, any times when the knee feels like it's giving way underneath you?"] Um, well, I, what do you mean by that? I'm not sure what, what that means.

[Clinician asked: "So, it means if you're, if you're there and uh, you just feel it sort of jolt, like give way, feel unstable underneath you."] Not, not bad, I mean it just kinda starts hurting, you know. Um.

[Clinician asked: "And does it hurt, uh, how far into the run does it generally hurt?"] You know I would say probably starts off like after, uh, maybe, twenty minutes or so. Which is, probably I don't know like somewhere around mile, three maybe,  two and a half or three miles.
[Clinician asked: "OK. Ooh you're fast. Um."] <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> switching my kilometres and miles, I'm not sure, all right.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> Um. And, um, do you have to stop running, or can you run through the pain?"] Yeah, that is a good question. So, um, you know the last few times, the last time I actually stopped, I mean I ran a little bit longer, but then I stopped, because it was um, I, I feel like it's getting worse.

[Clinician asked: "OK."] Um, and, when I start doing my abs, that's, you know when I do my abs. It's kind of requiring me to move my you know, um, to kind of go from the flex position on my knees, and straighten my legs, then I feel all this clicking.
[Clinician asked: "OK."] And, I think that's also freaked me out, and uh, I just figured you know, something's wrong here.
[Clinician asked: "Right. And, you said before, it's both knees."] So it's more, um, it's actually my right knee that hurts.
[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> And, any problems with your left one?"] No.

[Clinician asked: "OK, and, can you just describe in the knee, or show me whereabouts the pain, is happening mainly?"] So, ah, you know I, I feel it, it's kind of weird, I feel it in the middle, but I almost feel like maybe it kind of uh,  um, moves to the right, to the right of my knee a little bit.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Have you noticed any tenderness when you press over any areas in the knee?"] So, yes, well it's, I have, probably some fluid. I googled it, and you know it says that sometimes  fluid accumulates uh, I.
[Clinician asked: "OK. , does it look swollen to you?"] Sorry?
[Clinician asked: "Does the knee look swollen?"] Um, well it did, like, the first time I ran, it was a little bit swollen, but then I just put some like ice on it, and it went away.

[Clinician asked: "<INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>"] Um, but, right now, right now it's not swollen, and right now it doesn't hurt, you know. Um, but, it just kind of comes on when uh, um, or you know right now when I flex and extend my leg, it, there's no clicking but, I'll notice it in the gym and then, you know, when I run it'll start hurting.
[Clinician asked: "OK, and, if you're sitting, still for a while, do you have any problems when you stand up, any locking up of the knee?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "Can't straighten it. Any problems going up and down stairs?"] No.
[Clinician asked: "Any problems just walking normally?"] No, none, I don't.
[Clinician asked: "And intervals of range of movement. Are you able to fully straighten the leg?"] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "And what about fully flex it?"] Yes.

[Clinician asked: "OK. Are you taking any medication for the knee?"] Well, um, since you're asking. Um, yes I , I take Ibuprofen, um, and I'll just kinda, use it sometime like uh, um, like sometimes I have some joint issues, but you know, that kind of, is not very frequent. So I'll take Ibuprofen for that. But you know it's nothing like major maybe, like, four hundred milligrams max, something like that. I also sometimes will um, you know, smoke a joint. Um, and, yeah, other than that, that's it.

[Clinician asked: "OK, and any other joint problems, that you're getting? Any back pains? Small swelling of your, hands, feet, elbows, wrists? Any pain?"] No. Uh, well I have sometimes, you know how sometimes like if you stand for a long time, your feet will kinda, or your legs will swell up. Like I noticed it because of my socks, I'll just kind of, notice this indentation.

[Clinician asked: "OK."] And then, another weird thing. I've felt this just once, but there was this tingling in my toes, like uh, um, after the run. There was tingling in my toes like on my, you know my right toes, um, so.
[Clinician asked: "On your right side."] Yes.
[Clinician asked: "Any change in colour, or temperature of your lower legs?"] No.

[Clinician asked: "Any swelling which stays if you push in, and then you release it, that it stays there?"] Well I don't know if I really try that, but I, I will tell you that, what I noticed is like if I, you know when my socks come off, I kind of notice that indentation a little bit, I mean it's not like major, right but, I. Uh, I mean do you mean I just need to like press and see what happens, if it like kind of stays or, .
[Clinician asked: "Yeah, any, or any pain in the back of the calf or anything like that?"] No, no, nothing like that, no.

[Clinician asked: "Um, and, with your foot, has that gone back to normal now? Do you feel any altered senstation, tingling numbness, anything like that?"] No it was just, it was that time, uh, but it was just really weird, because it kind of went on for a while.
[Clinician asked: "OK.And any pain in the joints of your foot or , on the underside in the arch? Any problems walking round on hard floors?"] No. No.

[Clinician asked: "OK, have you had, any other medical problems before?"] Uh, well, I had uh. You know what just, kind of your garden variety stuff, right, colds and, uh. I mean I, I do have, I'm far-sighted, and so I had uh, I had Lasik surgery. And then, once I had this really bad UTI, they thought it was kidney stones, and so they had me actually in ER. And if my GP didn't call them, tell them, that you know, they don't need to give me Morphine. Um, that was kind of an awful experience but, uh, other than that, you know, I'm, I'm, I'm pretty good. <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/>

[Clinician asked: "OK, and, do you take any other medications, apart from the ones you told me about?"] Well, I'll take uh, I take vitamin D. Uh, I take melatonin. Sometimes, you know, just for cramps, I'll take, like, uh, um, ibuprofen, or actually , uh, I'll take, what am I taking? Aleve. Whatever Aleve is. Think that's maybe um, Acetaminophen, or, I can't remember what class of drugs. You know occasionally, if I'm really having hard times, like I'll, I'll sleeping, I'll take Xanax. Um, but, I prefer Melatonin, it's just sometimes Melatonin doesn't work.
[Clinician asked: "OK. And, any problems with your stomach, or asthma, indigestion, acid reflux?"] No, no, no problems.

[Clinician asked: ". And, what I would suggest is, definitely carrying on with the ice therapy that you've been using. So, as much as you can, 'cause that really does make a difference to injuries. Brings the healing blood into the area, and it gives it the best chance of, getting better. So, as much as you can, use t"] Mm-hmm.

[Clinician asked: "Um, obviously you're trying to stick to a marathon programme, but. Um, I would, try and ease up on the running, if you can. And if it, if it's painful enough that you're, having to stop running, then it's not a good idea, to do that. Um. It's fine to take the Ibuprofen. Um, I can prescribe you a sli"] Hmm.
[Clinician asked: "But make sure that with food. Ultimately we probably need to, examine the knee."] I see.

[Clinician asked: "Um, so, the best thing I would, suggest, getting a appointment with the physiotherapist first, who can do like a long, assessment, and examination of the knee. And then we can make an assessment as to whether we need to do any, um, imagery to , have a look inside the knee, and see if there's any pro"] OK. Well, you know I read, I read some stuff online that, that maybe like my ligaments there's, I think it's like ACL or, or something like that right, and so if that. I mean I'm kind of like freaked out that that may break. So do you think it's that, or?

[Clinician asked: "Um, it's, it's not really possible to, diagnose that over the , over the video. So we definitely would need to, see you. And, the, a physio is the best point of call, because they'll probably be the most likely initial treatment."] Right.
[Clinician asked: "But certainly if the appointment time's too long to wait, then we can see, we can see you in our clinic as well."] OK. OK. Can you, so are you going to send referral for that?

[Clinician asked: "Yep,  we can, we can do a referral before that. If in the meantime you develop like persistant swelling, heat, redness, um, of the knee, or high temperature, feel unwell, or it completely locks up and you can't bend and stretch it."] OK. Great.

[Clinician asked: "Then, you'd need to see a doctor urgently, so call us back <INAUDIBLE_SPEECH/> in that, case. ."] Mm-hmm. That sounds good. Thank you yeah. , sure I mean, give me the, stronger medicine, I'll give that a try.
[Clinician asked: "OK. I'll send it through to your pharmacy for you. OK? Thank you, bye."] OK. Sweet. Thanks so much. OK. Bye, bye.
gold_note:
PC: R knee pain

HPC: 2/52 R knee pain. Training for marathon. Currently up to 7-8 miles few times a week. Gym few x week. Had to stop running at 20 min point due to pain this week. 
Pain in front of knee and right side. No specific area tenderness. Has noticed puffiness of knee after exercise, used ice and this settled completely. No giving way/locking. No problems going up/downstairs or on normal walking. 
FROM. 
Generally feels pain is getting worse. 
Has had previous ice hockey injury and bruising afterwards.
No other jt pains, no general unwell/fever.
Has had tingling R side forefoot which settled down. Slight ankle swelling, indentation socks. No change in temp/colour.
No problems walking hard floors/pain in sole.

PMH:  LASIK, ? kidney stones.

DHx: Vit D, melatonin, ibuprofen. Xanax sometimes . 

SH: Has occ joint. 

Plan: Advised replace ibuprofen with naproxen which I will prescribe. Continue using ice as much as possible and reduce exercise. Do not continue to run with pain. No hx GI sx/asthma. Advise I will refer for physio for assessment and ongoing Rx. If persistent swelling/heat/redness/locking to see Dr asap.

gold_sections.HIGHLIGHTS:
Training for marathon
puffiness of knee after exercise
No giving way/locking
FROM
tingling R side forefoot
If persistent swelling/heat/redness/locking to see Dr asap

naive.response_text:
Mary Jo, thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It sounds like you're in discomfort, and I completely understand why you're worried. Your knee pain and the clicking sensation, especially during your training, are valid concerns. I want to reassure you that we're taking this seriously and will work together to get you back on track.

Based on our conversation, I recommend continuing with the ice therapy you've been using, as it helps reduce inflammation. It's also important to ease up on your running for a bit to allow your knee to heal. I can prescribe a stronger pain reliever if needed, and I'll arrange a referral to a physiotherapist who can assess your knee in more detail. In the meantime, if you experience any severe swelling, redness, or if you feel unwell, please reach out immediately.

Take care, and let me know if you have any questions or need further assistance. You're doing the right thing by addressing this early, and I'm here to support you through this.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Mary Jo, thank you for sharing your concerns with me. It’s completely understandable that you’re worried about your knee pain and the tingling in your toes, especially as you’re training for a marathon. I can see how this must be affecting your ability to continue with your training, and I want to work together to figure out what’s going on.

From what you’ve described, it sounds like the knee pain and clicking could be related to overuse from your training. Sometimes, increased activity can lead to inflammation or minor injuries in the knee joint. The tingling in your toes is a bit unusual and could be due to nerve irritation, though it’s also possible it’s unrelated. To be thorough, we should consider whether there might be any connection between your knee symptoms and the tingling, perhaps through referred pain or nerve compression.

Given this, I think it would be helpful to start with some imaging, like an MRI, to get a clearer picture of what’s going on inside your knee. This can help rule out any serious issues like ligament tears. In the meantime, I recommend continuing with the ice therapy you’ve been using, as it can reduce inflammation and help with healing. Adjusting your training routine to include more low-impact activities like swimming or cycling might also give your knees a chance to recover.

We’ll schedule a follow-up appointment in two weeks to check on your progress and discuss the results of any imaging. Before you leave, could you summarize the plan we’ve discussed so far? That way, I can make sure everything is clear and address any questions you might have.

full.response_text:
I can hear how much this has been worrying you — you said, "something's wrong here" and "freaked me out." That kind of worry is totally understandable, especially with the symptoms you're experiencing.

Knee pain, especially when running, can be caused by a variety of issues. Given your training for a marathon and gym routine, it's important to check for possible overuse injuries or alignment issues. We'll focus on identifying the exact cause so we can address it effectively.

Tingling in the toes can sometimes be related to nerve issues or circulation problems. We'll need to look into this further to see if there's an underlying cause that needs attention.

Abdominal clicking during exercises like crunches can be common, but it's worth checking to make sure it's not related to muscle strain or something else that needs treatment.

Our goal is to work together to figure out what's causing these symptoms. By addressing each concern step by step, we can develop a plan that makes sense for you.

To help with your knee pain, we'll start by understanding the underlying cause. This might involve looking at your running habits and any possible overuse patterns.

Addressing the tingling in your toes will involve checking for any nerve-related issues or circulation problems. We'll make sure to prioritize this as part of our overall plan.

We'll start by scheduling some tests to get a clearer picture of what's causing your knee pain and tingling.

In the meantime, we'll review your exercise routine to see if any adjustments can help reduce your knee pain.

We'll also monitor your abdominal clicking to ensure it doesn't become a bigger issue.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to check in again in two weeks. If you experience any worsening symptoms or new issues, please reach out sooner.
